title: Shattering the Symmetry Trap in Fixed-Ansatz VQE: An Accelerated ADAPT-VQE Study of Three Pillar Molecules under Bravyi-Kitaev Mapping
abstract: Fixed-ansatz Variational Quantum Eigensolvers (VQE), such as the Unitary Coupled Cluster with Singles and Doubles (UCCSD) framework, frequently suffer from severe initialization paralyzation and zero-gradient traps when evaluated using the non-local Bravyi-Kitaev (BK) fermion-to-qubit mapping. In this work, we systematically demonstrate how the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter (ADAPT-VQE) framework shatters these structural limitations across three distinct electronic and geometric molecular pillars: Lithium Hydride ($\text{LiH}$), Hydrogen Fluoride ($\text{HF}$), and Water ($\text{H}_2\text{O}$), under heavily stretched or asymmetric multi-reference configurations. While conventional UCCSD-VQE flatlines completely at a zero energy shift ($0.000000$~Ha) due to global phase cancellations inherent to the BK tree structures, our dynamic ADAPT-VQE loop successfully isolates the dominant symmetry-breaking operators using analytical commutator gradients. To bypass the severe $\mathcal{O}(N^3)$ computational bottlenecks of dense matrix exponentiation and Singular Value Decomposition on larger registers, we implement a highly optimized, vector-based Taylor series expansion state-evolution engine. Our numerical results show that the accelerated ADAPT-VQE framework achieves instant, exact Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) convergence within the very first macro-cycle across all three molecular systems, maintaining absolute numerical stability up to a 12-qubit register space. This study establishes a robust, hardware-efficient path for simulating strongly correlated, highly polarized triatomic chemical environments on near-term local architectures.
summary: The paper addresses the failure of standard VQE methods when simulating complex molecules using the Bravyi-Kitaev mapping due to global phase cancellations. By implementing an advanced ADAPT-VQE strategy, the authors demonstrate a robust method that instantly converges to the exact FCI energy for $	ext{LiH}$, $	ext{HF}$, and $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$ while maintaining computational efficiency up to 12 qubits.
main_problem: Fixed-ansatz VQE methods fail catastrophically when simulating strongly correlated molecular systems using non-local mappings like Bravyi-Kitaev, getting trapped in zero-gradient plateaus.
main_result: The ADAPT-VQE framework successfully overcomes these structural limitations by dynamically selecting symmetry-breaking operators, achieving instant convergence to the exact Full Configuration Interaction (FCI) energy in a single step for multiple molecules.
method: The study employs the Adaptive Derivative-Assembled Pseudo-Trotter (ADAPT-VQE) algorithm combined with an accelerated, vector-based Taylor series expansion engine to simulate quantum evolution efficiently on near-term hardware.
model_or_system: The system models are three small molecules ($	ext{LiH}$, $	ext{HF}$, and $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$) studied under highly stretched or asymmetric multi-reference electronic configurations, mapped onto a qubit register via the Bravyi-Kitaev transformation.
key_observables: Energy gradient ($g_k$), convergence to FCI energy floor (e.g., $-25.035892$ Ha for $	ext{HF}$), and detection of 'ghost gradients' as artifacts.
important_parameters: 12-qubit register space, 5th-order Taylor expansion order, and the specific molecular geometries ($	ext{LiH}$, $	ext{HF}$, $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$).
main_assumptions: The primary assumption is that ADAPT can dynamically isolate the necessary symmetry-breaking operators to bypass global phase cancellations inherent in the BK mapping structure.
figures_summary: Logs (Appendix A) demonstrate initial zero-gradient traps for fixed methods, followed by successful energy convergence after operator addition via the ADAPT cycle for all three molecules.
paper_structure: The paper diagnoses the failure of standard UCCSD-VQE with BK mapping; it then introduces and validates the ADAPT-VQE framework using commutator gradients; finally, it details computational optimizations (Taylor series) and presents convergence results across $	ext{LiH}$, $	ext{HF}$, and $	ext{H}_2	ext{O}$.
why_interesting: This work provides a crucial algorithmic breakthrough for applying quantum computation to chemistry by solving the fundamental problem of initialization paralysis in strongly correlated electronic structure calculations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
